paragraphs.zh: 所以我们实际上构建了一个 agent（智能体），它不仅会审查我们发出的每一次 code release（代码发布），还会审查每一次 configuration change（配置变更）。也就是说，如果你进入 Cloudflare dashboard（控制台），按下一个按钮，就会生成一个文件，然后这个文件会被推送到我们网络的其他部分。现在我们有一个 agent 在检查所有这些文件、每一次代码发布，而且它是基于十年的 incident（事故）数据训练出来的。我们有一些重大的公开事故，会登上新闻；但除此之外，随着时间推移，其实一直还有一种小事故构成的“背景噪音”。我们当时有一个几乎让人倒吸一口气的时刻：负责我们基础设施工程团队的一个人，在全员会上做展示，他说，好，这里是我们事故的背景噪音，然后这里突然断崖式下降，接着就变成这样了。他说，你们觉得那里发生了什么？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And that was the moment that we released this agent that's checking in.

I think one of the things that people don't appreciate and don't talk about enough with AI is, yeah, AI is you know good at replacing a bunch of tasks that the humans do and it doesn't tire and it doesn't need breaks and it doesn't you know have have have to sleep.
But the really interesting thing is it's also uncorrelated to all the other biases that are within an organization.
A team of people working together will tend to develop a set of biases and that doesn't mean that AI doesn't have biases, but they're uncorrelated with the rest of the team.

And so it's actually incredibly good at measuring things and what I think you're gonna see is that if it's gonna seem like software is incredibly scary for the next two years and then it'll kind of just fade away and we're gonna all be releasing software which is so much better than it ever has been before and so it's like our uptime and our reliability and our performance has gone up you know an order of magnitude over the course of the last year and it's all because we're using agents in order to more securely you know build build our systems and I think everyone is is going to be doing that going forward.

paragraphs.zh: 那就是我们发布这个检查流程 agent 的时刻。我觉得人们没有充分意识到、也没有被足够多讨论的一点是：AI（人工智能）当然擅长替代人类做的一大堆任务，而且它不会累，不需要休息，也不需要睡觉。但真正有意思的是，它和一个组织内部其他偏见之间是“不相关”的。一个一起工作的团队，往往会逐渐形成一套共同偏见；这并不是说 AI 没有偏见，但它的偏见和团队其他人的偏见并不相关。所以它实际上特别擅长做测量和校验。我认为你将会看到的是：software 在接下来的两年里会显得极其吓人，然后这种感觉会慢慢淡去，而我们最终发布的软件会比过去任何时候都更好。所以你知道，在过去一年里，我们的 uptime（在线率）、reliability（可靠性）和 performance（性能）都提升了一个 order of magnitude（数量级），而这一切都是因为我们在用 agents（智能体）以更安全的方式来构建我们的系统。我觉得接下来每个人都会这么做。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 2

sentences.text: We are on the on that topic let's go into how you guys use AI internally.

You have some interesting stats that you published, just, a few weeks ago in April, where you said the company has 93% of R and D employees, using AI coding tools, 3,683 internal users, whatever $241,000,000,000 tokens.
How did you get there?
How did you create that culture for a company that was started in 2009?
And '2, how do you measure success?

paragraphs.zh: 既然说到这个话题，我们就来聊聊你们内部是怎么使用 AI 的。几周前，也就是 4 月，你们公布了一些很有意思的数据：公司里 93% 的 R&D（研发）员工都在使用 AI coding tools（AI 编码工具），有 3,683 名内部用户，还有 241,000,000,000 个 token（词元）。你们是怎么走到这一步的？对于一家创立于 2009 年的公司，你们是如何建立起这种文化的？第二，你们又是如何衡量成功的？

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: We were selling picks and shovels and sort of the AI Gold Rush but we were actually pretty cautious users ourselves.
We'd run experiments but but we were sort of not kind of all in on it and we had a bunch of people internally that were that were skeptical.
A guy named Kenton Varda who built the Workers' Platform, this incredible engineer.
You know, we're not supposed to talk about 10x engineers but Kenton is clearly a 10x engineer.
He's just he's he's he's he's just a genius.

paragraphs.zh: 我们当时是在 AI Gold Rush（AI 淘金热）里卖 picks and shovels（镐和铲）的人，但实际上我们自己用得相当谨慎。我们会做实验，但还没有真正彻底 all in（全力投入）。而且公司内部有不少人对此持怀疑态度。有个叫 Kenton Varda 的人，他打造了 Workers Platform，这是一位非常了不起的工程师。你知道，现在大家不太该谈什么 10x engineer（十倍工程师），但 Kenton 显然就是个 10x engineer。他就是，他就是，他真的是个天才。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: And he, you know, last in the spring of twenty twenty five, he's like this ASF is is BS.
I'm gonna go play with all of these tools and show you just how much of a mess they they are and why why why we shouldn't be relying on them.
And and he did, he went away.
He was super senior guy on our team, went away for a month played with him came back and he was like, oh my god, I am now a 100 times more productive than I was before.
Which is which if that's right and he's a 10x engineer means that he alone was more productive than our entire team was back in like 2019.

paragraphs.zh: 你知道，他在 2025 年春天时还觉得，这个 ASF 简直就是 BS。他当时说，我要去把这些工具都玩一遍，给你们看看它们到底有多乱，以及为什么我们不该依赖它们。然后他真这么做了。他是我们团队里资历非常深的人，离开去研究了一个月，回来之后他说，天哪，我现在的生产力是以前的 100 倍。如果这是真的，而他本来就是个 10x engineer，那就意味着光他一个人，生产力就比我们整个团队在 2019 年时还高。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: Right?
And so, and and like I kind of believe that's true.
Like it's it's it was it was remarkable.

And so internally on the engineering side, I think it spread pretty quickly and I think the real turning point for us was probably November 2025 where it felt like and it was with the release of Claude Opus and and and a bunch of things but it felt like it it tipped where we all of a sudden our engineering side we're getting you know much much much more productive.
I think that's the story everyone that's sort of where everyone is today and everyone is seeing some version of that.

paragraphs.zh: 对吧？所以，而且我某种程度上相信这是真的。那真的非常惊人。所以在内部的 engineering（工程）这边，我觉得这种变化传播得非常快。而我认为对我们来说真正的转折点大概是在 2025 年 11 月，当时随着 Claude Opus 的发布，以及其他一系列事情，感觉整个局面发生了倾斜——突然之间，我们 engineering 这边的生产力高了非常非常非常多。我觉得这也是现在所有人的共同故事，大致就是今天每个人所处的位置，每个人都在看到某种版本的这种变化。

paragraphs.speaker: Speaker 1

sentences.text: I will say that you know for us you know, we've there there have been the people that I continue to worry about are the ones that have been very hesitant to embrace these tools and it tends to be not the super senior folks, not the super junior folks, it tends to be the people kind of in the middle of their career and it makes some sense where they they've been doing well in the world that existed.

They've been told this is the game to play and if you play this game you're gonna succeed And so they they've played the game and now someone comes along and says we're changing the rules of the game on you.
And that feels deeply deeply deeply unfair.

And yet again you kind of have to adjust to what's out there but I I really do worry that we're going to have this sort of lost generation of sort of either, you know, young Millennials or older Gen Z folks that just they were sort of they're gonna have incentives to say we shouldn't use these tools because again if they do they don't feel like they have any advantage of the people who are coming from behind them.

paragraphs.zh: 我想说的是，对我们来说，我一直担心的其实是那些非常犹豫、不愿意拥抱这些工具的人。而这类人往往不是最资深的人，也不是最初级的人，往往是那些处在职业生涯中段的人。这其实也说得通，因为他们在原本那个世界里一直做得不错。别人一直告诉他们，这就是该玩的游戏，只要你按这个游戏规则来，你就会成功。所以他们就一直按这套规则在玩。结果现在突然有人来说，我们要改游戏规则了。这种感觉会让人觉得非常非常非常不公平。但话说回来，你还是得去适应外部现实。我确实担心，我们会出现某种“失落的一代”——大概是较年轻的 Millennials 或年纪稍长的 Gen Z——他们会天然有动机说我们不该使用这些工具，因为一旦用了，他们就会觉得自己相比后面追上来的人不再有任何优势。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 59:30 - 1:00:39 And so a lot of what I've been trying to do is how do you bring those groups groups along.
The other piece was, know, I think engineering teams are very natural users of these these products and and coding has been a place where you've gotten really clear value from them.
But like that's just a small part of Cloudflare.
We've got, you know, legal, finance, know, marketing, sales, all of these other organizations that are part of the team that were like how can we make them significantly more productive as well.

And so we came up with this idea that you know, could we create a sort of a user friendly system that allowed you know someone on the finance team to be able to you know do their job incredibly incredibly efficiently And and we called it Cloudflare OS and it's it's it's it's basically it's a harness where we've taken a bunch of things that have to be true and gotten them into it.

paragraphs.zh: 所以我一直在努力做的，很大一部分就是：怎么把这些群体也一起带上。另一部分是，我觉得 engineering 团队本来就是这些产品非常天然的用户，而且 coding（编码）一直是这些工具能够提供非常明确价值的场景。但那其实只是 Cloudflare 的一小部分。我们还有 legal（法务）、finance（财务）、marketing（市场）、sales（销售），以及所有这些其他团队组织。我们当时想的是，怎样也能让他们的生产力显著提升。所以我们提出了一个想法：能不能做出一个更 user friendly（用户友好）的系统，让 finance 团队里的人也能把工作做得极其极其高效。我们把它叫做 Cloudflare OS。它本质上是一个 harness（工作框架），我们把一系列必须成立的条件都整合进去了。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:00:39 - 1:01:38 We've taken a bunch of things that are sort of just informative based on our experience and then we've taught it skills where it can do various things.

There's nothing that's magical about what we did except that, you know we had a pretty significant advantage because we had all the security tooling where we could hook into all the different systems of record we have, the ERP system we have, you know sales systems we have, workplace management systems that we have and do it in a way that was secure and where an agent working on behalf of a user could in could inherit the the specific kind of access that that user had.
And I think that's where a lot of these things fall down is where people don't have like that security model in place.
We did and so we were able to build it pretty quickly.
The other thing that was was where I think people fall down is if you ask someone to describe that what they do in their job, they'll describe it but they'll leave like half of their job out.

paragraphs.zh: 我们把很多基于经验、主要起信息作用的东西放了进去，然后又教给它一些 skills（技能），让它能够执行各种任务。我们做的事情本身并没有什么神奇之处，唯一特别的是，我们有一个相当明显的优势：我们拥有完整的 security（安全）工具链，因此可以接入我们各种不同的 systems of record（记录系统）、我们的 ERP system、我们的销售系统、我们的 workplace management systems（工作场所管理系统），并且以一种安全的方式来完成这一切。这样一来，一个代表用户工作的 agent（代理）就可以继承该用户本身所拥有的那种特定访问权限。我认为很多这类系统失败，恰恰就是因为人们没有建立起那样的 security model（安全模型）。而我们有，所以我们能够很快把它搭起来。另一个我觉得大家容易出问题的地方是：如果你让一个人描述他在工作中做什么，他会描述，但通常会漏掉大概一半的工作内容。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:01:38 - 1:02:06 And so the question is how do you capture really what are the jobs to be done within an organization?
And so the clever thing is a guy named Sam Ray on our team and he was like listen we're gonna set up an email address and we're gonna tell everyone that it's a magic AI agent.
So for us it was autocloudflow dot com and and we told everyone it's a magic AI agent.
We've set it up.
You can ask it anything.

paragraphs.zh: 所以问题就在于：你要怎样真正捕捉一个组织内部那些“需要完成的工作”到底是什么？我们团队里一个叫 Sam Ray 的人想了个很聪明的办法。他说，听着，我们要设一个 email address（邮箱地址），然后告诉所有人，这是一个神奇的 AI agent。对我们来说，那个地址是 autocloudflow dot com。我们告诉所有人，这是个神奇的 AI agent，我们已经把它搭好了，你可以问它任何问题。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:02:07 - 1:03:04 Now in reality behind the scenes, it was a team of about 20 people.

The staff this 20 fourseven and yes, were using AI tools, what they were really doing was gathering kind of the the information about what are the real problems that people have in their jobs and then they were building that in and turning it into a set of skill files and information to be done.

And so an example of this is there's a woman named Heather who's on our Investor Relations team and so we're a public company and so every time we have earnings, it's about two weeks of prep ahead of time to generate all the IR documents and and and everything that that needs to be done and it's it's kind of arduous kind of drudgerous work to put this all all together And Heather's like, I think I can automate it all.
And so we went from what used to be a two week process down to what is now a three minute process.
It is more accurate.

paragraphs.zh: 而实际上，在幕后是一个大约 20 人的团队。他们 24/7 轮班支持。没错，他们也在用 AI 工具，但他们真正做的，是收集信息，弄清楚大家在工作中真正遇到的问题是什么，然后把这些内容逐步构建进去，把它们变成一组 skill files（技能文件）和可执行的信息。举个例子，我们 Investor Relations（投资者关系）团队里有位女士叫 Heather。因为我们是一家上市公司，所以每次到财报季之前，大约都要提前两周准备，生成所有 IR 文件以及其他所有需要完成的内容。这项工作相当繁琐，也很枯燥，要把这些东西全部整理出来并不轻松。Heather 说，我觉得我可以把它全部自动化。于是，我们把原本需要两周的流程，缩短到了现在只要三分钟。而且结果还更准确。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:03:04 - 1:04:03 We actually found errors in in things that we had put up before nothing nothing significant but you know little little things here and there and now we're able to take that and that means that then our IR team can spend more time meeting with investors, talking with you know our shareholders, doing all the things that are actually the really productive parts of their job and much less time actually generating those documents.

And so I think that that every company is going to be going through this and it's interesting we again we we get questions like, how are you guys doing this and we keep showing off this Cloudflare OS thing.

I was I was on the I'm on the organizing part part of the the group that's organizing the twenty thirty four Olympics in in Utah and I was talking to the guy named Milario who's the CTO of the IOC and he's like, listen we need this we need a system to do this and I was like, well let me show you what we're doing at Cloudflare and and so I showed it to him.
We never thought this was gonna be a product.

paragraphs.zh: 我们实际上还发现了以前做出来的一些东西里的错误，不是什么重大错误，但你知道，就是这里一点、那里一点的小问题。现在我们能够把这部分工作交给系统处理，这意味着我们的 IR 团队就能花更多时间去见投资者、和股东交流，去做那些真正属于他们工作中高价值、高生产力的部分，而不是把大量时间花在生成这些文档上。所以我觉得，每一家公司都会经历这个过程。有意思的是，我们也不断被问到，你们到底是怎么做到这些的？而我们总是在展示这个 Cloudflare OS 的东西。我当时在 Utah 参与一个负责组织 2034 Olympics 的小组，我也在那个组织团队里。我和一个叫 Milario 的人聊过，他是 IOC 的 CTO。他说，听着，我们需要这个，我们需要一个能做这件事的系统。我当时就说，那我给你看看我们在 Cloudflare 是怎么做的。于是我就展示给他看。我们从来没想过这会成为一个产品。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:04:04 - 1:04:44 And he's like, I need you in Luzon, you know, next week to and to and bring your team because we need to implement this.
And so we all flew up and now they're actually using this to, you know, to help break better organized Olympic Games.
And and I think that that if you get and every organization is gonna have to build something like this.
And if you do it in a way which is secure that gives users access, again, I think it's gonna it's gonna just massively empower teams in order to to be successful.
I I'm not sort of in the sort of the I guess the Dario camp of, you know, AI is gonna replace all jobs.

paragraphs.zh: 他当时说，我需要你们下周去 Luzon，把你的团队也带上，因为我们需要把这个东西实施起来。于是我们就都飞过去了。现在他们实际上已经在用这个系统，来帮助更好地组织 Olympic Games。我认为，如果你把这件事做成——而且每个组织最终都得构建类似的东西——并且你是以一种既安全、又能让用户获得访问权限的方式来做，我还是觉得，这会极大地赋能团队，帮助他们取得成功。我并不属于那种——我猜可以说是 Dario 那一派——认为 AI 会取代所有工作的观点。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:04:44 - 1:05:17 I think it is gonna replace a certain set of jobs like I think we're gonna like if I have a developer and I can now give them AI tools and they're 10 times as productive, I'm gonna hire as many developers as I can.
If I you know, I'm a salesperson and I can give them tools that eliminate the parts of their job that they don't like which is coming up with account plans and and doing all of those things.
What they want is to be in front of customers and and out there selling.
I'm not as many salespeople as I as I can.
Where I think there's going to be a big change is the parts of the organization that that aren't building and they aren't selling, they're basically measuring.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得它确实会取代某一类工作。比如说，如果我有一个 developer（开发者），现在我可以给他配上 AI 工具，让他的生产力提升 10 倍，那我就会尽可能多招 developer。如果我是做销售的，我可以给 salesperson（销售人员）提供工具，把他们工作中那些自己不喜欢的部分消掉，比如制定 account plan、处理那些杂事。他们真正想做的是站到客户面前，出去卖东西。那样的话，我也会尽可能多招 salespeople。我认为真正会发生巨大变化的，是组织里那些既不负责构建、也不负责销售，而基本上只是负责衡量、监督的部分。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:05:18 - 1:06:17 And it turns out that AI systems are much better measures than than any humans again in part because they're uncorrelated and I and so I think a lot of those things are gonna be the case.

Another example internally, you know, audit which is again one of these things that you do when you're a public company, you probably don't do it when you're small startup but you do you do it eventually.

We have a 105 or something risk areas and every quarter historically the internal audit team has picked six to 10 of them and then they do sort of these deep dive audits to make sure that we're you know not screwing up procurement or we're doing revenue recognition correctly or you know and they're measuring effectively the organization.
We're now getting to a place with these tools where we can take all 105 of those risk areas and continuously audit them and find any issues that are there.
And I think that that's like that's making us a better organization.

paragraphs.zh: 而事实证明，AI 系统在“衡量”这件事上比任何人类都强，部分原因还是因为它们彼此不相关联（uncorrelated）。所以我觉得，其中很多事情都会变成这样。再举一个内部的例子：audit（审计）。这同样是那种当你成为一家 public company（上市公司）后就必须做的事；你还是小 startup（初创公司）时可能不会做，但迟早会做。我们大概有 105 个左右的风险领域，过去每个季度，内部审计团队都会从里面挑 6 到 10 个，然后做这种深度审计，确保我们没有把 procurement（采购）搞砸，或者 revenue recognition（收入确认）做得正确，诸如此类。他们本质上是在衡量整个组织。现在借助这些工具，我们正走到这样一个阶段：可以把这 105 个风险领域全部纳入进来，持续不断地审计它们，并找出其中存在的任何问题。我觉得这会让我们成为一个更好的组织。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:06:17 - 1:06:30 It's freeing up people to be able to work on either building or selling which are really where value is created within organizations.
And it's just incredible to see how much more productive the teams are.

paragraphs.zh: 这也把人解放出来，让他们可以去做构建或销售，而这两件事才是真正在组织内部创造价值的地方。看到团队的生产力提升了这么多，真的非常惊人。

sentences.text: Speaker 2 | 1:06:30 - 1:06:44 And do you think that there is a painful transition period from the current world to the next world?
So do you think everybody's gonna have to go through this as organizations recalibrate?
We laid off

paragraphs.zh: 那你觉得，从当前这个世界走向下一个世界，中间会不会有一个痛苦的过渡期？也就是说，随着组织重新校准，你觉得每个人都得经历这个过程吗？我们裁掉了——

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:06:44 - 1:07:29 more than 20% of our team and not because the business was struggling, but and not because they weren't great team members.
But we just need fewer middle managers, we need fewer measures and those roles were going away.
As I talked to peers at other companies, they're all seeing the same thing and they're all saying yeah we're gonna have to do the same thing at some point and seeing these sort of trickle out.
But there's a real fear in leaders out there that they don't want to be the first ones to do that.
And you know pardon the vernacular, but I think that's chicken shit because the cruelest thing that you can do is wait.

paragraphs.zh: ——超过 20% 的团队成员，这并不是因为业务出了问题，也不是因为他们不是很优秀的团队成员，而是因为我们需要更少的 middle managers（中层管理者），需要更少的“衡量者”，而这些岗位正在消失。我和其他公司的同行交流时，他们看到的情况都一样，他们也都在说，是的，我们在某个时点也不得不做同样的事，而且已经开始看到这种情况一点点扩散出去。但现在领导者中确实存在一种恐惧：他们不想成为第一个这么做的人。说句不太文雅的话，我觉得这很怂，因为你能做的最残酷的事，就是等待。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:07:31 - 1:08:55 I do think in the next six to twelve months almost every company is going to go through some exercise like this where they're going to cut a bunch of their team And I think a lot of people are waiting, a lot of CEOs are waiting around to do it because they're afraid that they're gonna look bad in the process.

What really struck me was the realization that once you know that that change is going to happen, the kindest thing that you can do for your your team is to do it as soon as possible because it's way easier to get a job today than it's going to be in six to twelve months because the market's going to get flooded.
And so we put together an incredibly, I mean the most generous severance package in tech that we've seen.

We let stock continue to vest which nobody does but we did And we're working incredibly hard to place these people because they're incredible people and somebody who is a middle manager at Cloudflare can be a senior manager at a startup and do an extraordinary job.

But but once you're as an organization, you've realized you're going to do it, like the the kindest thing that you can do for your team is to do it as soon as possible because what the worst thing that can happen is that there's just a bloodbath six to twelve months from now where everyone does it at the same time and the market gets flooded with a bunch of people who are all of a sudden looking looking for jobs.

paragraphs.zh: 我的确认为，在接下来的六到十二个月里，几乎每家公司都会经历某种类似的动作，大幅裁减一部分团队成员。我觉得很多人都在等，很多 CEO 都在拖着不做，因为他们害怕自己在这个过程中看起来不好看。真正让我深受触动的是这样一个认识：一旦你已经知道这种变化一定会发生，那你能为团队做的最善意的事，就是尽早去做。因为今天找工作，要比六到十二个月后容易得多；到那时，市场会被大量求职者淹没。所以我们拿出了一套非常、非常慷慨的离职补偿方案——我的意思是，这是我们所见过科技行业里最慷慨的 severance package（遣散补偿方案）。我们允许股票继续 vest（归属），这一点几乎没人这么做，但我们做了。我们也在非常努力地帮助这些人找到去处，因为他们都是非常出色的人。在 Cloudflare 做 middle manager 的人，到了 startup 里完全可能成为 senior manager，而且表现得极其优秀。但一旦作为组织你已经意识到自己会走到这一步，那你能为团队做的最善意的事，就是尽快去做。因为最糟糕的情况就是，六到十二个月后出现一场“血洗”，所有人同时动手，市场一下子涌入一大批突然开始找工作的人。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:08:55 - 1:09:46 And so that was the cattle that was sort of the the rationale that went through us and and again I there were there are people who are really close friends of mine who we let go because the roles that they had just didn't make sense anymore and and we we wanted to do it in a significant enough way that we would never have to do it again.
And so we're I feel comfortable saying that for the foreseeable future, we're not gonna have to do anything like that again because we've reorganized the organization.
And I think that you know one of the metrics that that we look at is span of control.
And so how many direct reports, what's the average number of direct reports in an in an organization?
And you that the historic number that like the Harvard Business School did a study and they came up that the right number for that is about six.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，这大致就是我们当时的思路和背后的 rationale（逻辑）。而且再说一次，被我们裁掉的人里，有些其实是我非常亲近的朋友，只是他们所承担的那些角色已经不再合理、没有意义了。我们希望一次做得足够彻底，这样以后就再也不用做第二次。所以我可以比较有把握地说，在可预见的未来，我们不会再需要做类似的事了，因为我们已经把组织重组过了。我觉得我们会看的一个指标是 span of control（管理幅度）。也就是，一个组织里平均每位管理者有多少个 direct reports（直接下属）。历史上的那个数字——Harvard Business School 以前做过一项研究，他们得出的结论是，这个数字大约在 6 左右才是合适的。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:09:46 - 1:10:19 So for every manager they have six direct reports and that was exactly what we were at.
It's so clear that with these new tools a manager can manage a lot more people.
You know Meta is trying to get to 50 to one.

I think that's all that seems like too many to me but I think 12 to one is right and the benefit of expanding the number of the average number of direct reports is it inherently flattens the organization.
The fewer number of direct reports on average a manager has inherently the organization is going to be much more hierarchical.

paragraphs.zh: 所以，对每一位 manager 来说，他们有 6 个 direct reports（直接下属），而我们当时正好就是这个比例。现在已经非常清楚了：有了这些新工具，一个 manager 可以管理更多人。你知道 Meta 现在在尝试做到 50 比 1。我觉得那看起来还是太多了，但我认为 12 比 1 是合适的。提高平均 direct reports 数量的好处在于，它会天然地让组织更扁平。平均而言，一个 manager 的 direct reports 越少，组织就必然会越层级化。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:10:20 - 1:11:09 The more direct reports they have it's going to be flatter and I think one of the things that you know we're seeing is that the flatter the organization is the more the easier it is for us to move quickly to be able to deliver things and and again I think that's that's what's that's what's driven a lot of us but yeah I think this is coming across the industry and again it's not a statement that any person is a bad person or a bad individual.
In fact they're great but there are roles that you're just not going to need anymore and some people you're going to be able to sort of re home and reskill inside your organization.
But like if you're going to go from you know six to one to 12 to one, that that means a whole bunch of middle managers are gonna are gonna go away.

paragraphs.zh: 他们的 direct reports 越多，组织就会越扁平。我认为我们现在看到的一点是：组织越扁平，我们就越容易快速行动，越能够把东西交付出来。而且，我觉得这也确实是推动我们很多决策的原因之一。不过，这种情况正在整个行业里出现。再说一次，这并不是在说某个人不好，或者某个人不行。事实上，他们都很优秀；但有些 role（岗位）就是以后不再需要了。有些人你可以在组织内部重新安置、重新培训。但如果你要从 6 比 1 变成 12 比 1，那就意味着会有一大批 middle managers（中层管理者）消失。

sentences.text: Speaker 2 | 1:11:09 - 1:11:26 So what do you do if you're one of those people?

If you're a middle manager and I think earlier you mentioned old Gen Z or young millennial and you find yourself in that situation like mid career, do you just go down a complete rabbit hole of like just making yourself as AI peeled as possible?

paragraphs.zh: 那如果你正好是这类人，你该怎么办？如果你是 middle manager，而且我记得你前面提到过，可能是年纪稍大的 Gen Z 或者年轻的 millennial，然后你发现自己处在这种 mid-career（职业中期）的位置上，你是不是就得一头扎进这个兔子洞，尽可能让自己变得足够“AI 化”？

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:11:26 - 1:12:10 I think yes.
Recognize that it's natural for that to feel scary and that the and that your instinct is going to be to fight against it.
But that the more that you can embrace these tools the better.

I've never seen, you know, and again running this now for seventeen years, like I've never seen more senior managers on our team say raise their hand and say, hey I want to go back to being an individual contributor.
And so we're rethinking like even how compensation works and everything else because I think that the power of an individual contributor and the amount of work that they can do is is extraordinary.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得是的。首先要认识到，觉得害怕是很自然的，而且你的本能反应会是抗拒它。但你越能拥抱这些工具，就越好。你知道，我做这件事已经 17 年了，在这段时间里，我从来没有见过这么多 senior managers（高级管理者）主动举手说：“嘿，我想回去做 individual contributor（个人贡献者）。”所以我们甚至在重新思考 compensation（薪酬）机制以及其他一切，因为我认为，individual contributor 的能力，以及他们现在能够完成的工作量，都是非常惊人的。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:12:10 - 1:13:26 So I think that for us, you know, our most senior members of our team are embracing this because it's a way for them to reinvent themselves and they're senior enough that they're not scared about it.

Our most junior folks, so we we we hired 1,111 that number is meaningful to us, but interns this summer, you know and I mean it's like they're the most extraordinary humans because everybody stopped hiring interns which I think is just total mistake.

And so we got sort of the pick of the litter and and and they're and they're all totally AI native and what we're trying to do is get both of those groups to get the folks that have been more hesitant because the fact is 92% of organization engineering organizations using AI tools like that that should be a 100% for sure and and it's but you've got to get people who especially are sort of mid career to be brave enough to learn these new these new tricks and and I think that that's that's that's really important and that and again with the you can't have one person who is 10 or a 100 times as productive in the same role as somebody else making the same amount of money as somebody else who's who's sort of doing it the old way.

paragraphs.zh: 所以我觉得，对我们来说，团队里最资深的成员正在拥抱这一变化，因为这给了他们重新塑造自己的机会，而且他们资历够深，不会因此感到害怕。我们最初级的员工也是如此。这个夏天我们招了 1,111 名 interns（实习生），这个数字对我们是有特殊意义的。而且，他们真的是最出色的一群人，因为几乎所有公司都停止招实习生了，我觉得这完全是个错误。所以我们某种程度上拿到了最优秀的一批人，而且他们全都是彻底的 AI native（AI 原住民）。我们现在想做的是，把这两类人带动起来，也把那些更犹豫的人带起来。因为现实是，92% 的 engineering organizations（工程组织）都在使用 AI 工具，这个数字显然应该是 100%。但你必须让人们，尤其是处在 mid-career 的人，足够勇敢去学习这些新把戏。我认为这一点非常重要。再说一次，你不能让一个人在同一个 role 里，生产力是另一个人的 10 倍甚至 100 倍，却拿着和那个仍在用老方式工作的人一样多的钱。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:13:26 - 1:13:56 Like one of those two is gonna leave and the worst case is if the person who's a 100 times as productive leaves.

And so we're just we are continuously trying to get every way and lift everyone up and and again part of why we did the layoff was in order to be able to, you know, really really free up the resources and and catalyze the entire organization to be able to do this.
But and it's not just your developers, has to be finance, legal, marketing, everything has got to be embracing these new tools.

paragraphs.zh: 这两个人里，总会有一个离开；而最糟糕的情况，是那个生产力高出 100 倍的人离开。所以我们一直在想尽办法把每个人都带起来、都提升上来。再说一次，我们之所以裁员，部分原因也是为了真正释放资源，并催化整个组织去做到这一点。而且这不只是 developers（开发者）的事，finance、legal、marketing，所有部门都必须拥抱这些新工具。

sentences.text: Speaker 2 | 1:13:56 - 1:14:01 So almost a year ago now you declared I think that was Content

paragraphs.zh: 所以差不多一年前，你宣布了——我想那应该是 Content

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:14:01 - 1:14:03 Independence Day July 1.

paragraphs.zh: Independence Day，7 月 1 日。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:14:04 - 1:14:07 Coming up on the second anniversary or I guess first anniversary.

paragraphs.zh: Speaker 1 | 1:14:04 - 1:14:07 快到两周年了，或者我想应该说是一周年。

sentences.text: What happened in the last year?
How is that whole debate evolving?
Yeah.
So And what was it in the first place?

paragraphs.zh: Speaker 2 | 1:14:07 - 1:14:14 对。过去一年发生了什么？整个争论是怎么演变的？对。所以这件事最开始又是什么来着？

sentences.text: So so so again, I think that it is you've just seen an enormous rise in the amount of sort of automated traffic that's running across the Internet.
I think in five years that you'll that bots and agents will outnumber humans online a thousand to one.
At the same time, like the interface through which we access the Internet is changing and we've gone through other sort of platform changes before.
You know, we went from browsing the Internet on a browser on your desktop or laptop to social to mobile.

paragraphs.zh: Speaker 1 | 1:14:14 - 1:14:52 对。所以还是那句话，我认为你现在看到的是，互联网上运行的某种自动化流量（traffic）数量已经大幅上升。我认为五年后，在线的 bots 和 agents 数量会比人类多出一千比一。与此同时，我们访问互联网的 interface（界面）也在发生变化，而这种 platform（平台）变迁以前也发生过。你知道，我们经历过从在 desktop 或 laptop 上用 browser 浏览互联网，到 social，再到 mobile 的转变。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:14:52 - 1:15:46 But throughout all of those things, underlying business model of the Internet for the last twenty eight years has remained basically the same which is it's largely advertising and then to a lesser extent subscription driven.

The problem is that first, there's just gonna be a lot more cost to run on the Internet because you're gonna have a thousand times as much traffic which means you're gonna have to have servers and CPUs and network and all that stuff and and someone needs to, you know, pay for it.
And the business model of the last twenty eight years which largely advertising based doesn't work because bots don't click on ads.
And so something has to change and so a part of that is how do we make the Internet more efficient?
Like how do we take legacy software and port it to things that are, you know, can continue to scale in a cost effective way?

paragraphs.zh: Speaker 1 | 1:14:52 - 1:15:46 但贯穿这一切的是，过去二十八年来互联网底层的 business model（商业模式）基本没变，本质上主要还是 advertising 驱动，其次才是 subscription 驱动。问题在于，第一，互联网的运行成本会高得多，因为流量会变成现在的一千倍，这意味着你需要更多 servers、CPUs、network 以及所有这些基础设施，而总得有人为此买单。而过去二十八年那种主要基于 advertising 的 business model 行不通，因为 bots 不会点广告。所以一定有什么东西得改变。其中一部分就是，我们怎样让互联网变得更高效？比如，怎样把 legacy software 迁移到那些能够继续以成本有效方式扩展的东西上？

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:15:47 - 1:16:23 But another big part of that is how do we figure out what the future business model of the Internet is is is gonna look like.
And you know, we we have great relations with all the different AI labs and and and they get it.

And they actually you know they they say listen as long as it's fair as long as everybody has to do the same thing we get that we've got to pay for the content that we're using in order to build our systems and our and our and our models.
But we've got to build a system to do that.
And so like we're experimenting in a bunch of different ways.

paragraphs.zh: Speaker 1 | 1:15:47 - 1:16:23 但另一大部分是，我们怎样弄清楚互联网未来的 business model 会是什么样。你知道，我们和各家不同的 AI labs 关系都很好，他们也明白这一点。事实上他们会说，听着，只要这件事是公平的，只要每个人都必须遵守同样的规则，我们就理解：为了构建我们的 systems 和 models，我们必须为所使用的内容付费。但我们得先建立起这样一套 system 来实现这件事。所以我们正在用很多不同方式做实验。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:16:23 - 1:16:41 The first thing that we did was we said in order for a market to exist, know, you always think you have to have supply and demand.
It's not exactly right.
What you actually need to have is scarcity of supply and demand.
If everything is free then then no one pays for it.
Like we're sitting in this room, we're breathing the air, doesn't cost us anything.

paragraphs.zh: Speaker 1 | 1:16:23 - 1:16:41 我们做的第一件事是，我们说，要让一个 market 存在，通常你会觉得需要 supply 和 demand。其实不完全对。你真正需要的是供给的稀缺性以及 demand。如果一切都是免费的，那就没人会为它付钱。比如我们坐在这个房间里，呼吸空气，这对我们来说不花钱。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:16:42 - 1:17:12 On the other hand, we go scuba diving, air is scarce and so we have to pay for it and so you know that's that.
So step one was we said we need to create controls that allow the people who have content online to be able to say who can access it and who can't.
And again, it's everyone's going to make different decisions.
In our case, we want all the bots to be consuming our information.
We want that to be out there and that's probably half of our customers are in that camp where they actually want to make it as easy as possible.

paragraphs.zh: Speaker 1 | 1:16:42 - 1:17:12 但另一方面，如果我们去 scuba diving，空气就是稀缺的，所以我们必须为它付钱，大概就是这个道理。所以第一步是，我们说需要建立控制机制，让那些在线拥有内容的人能够决定谁可以访问、谁不可以访问。再说一次，每个人都会做出不同决定。以我们为例，我们希望所有 bots 都来消费我们的信息，我们希望这些信息被广泛传播，而且大概有一半客户也属于这一阵营——他们实际上想让这件事尽可能容易。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:17:12 - 1:18:19 And so we provide the tools to as cost effectively, as efficiently as possible get information and take take HTML which has all this croft around it converted into markdown which is a much more streamlined system and then as a result like you can get more information in these AI systems without blowing up their context windows and all kinds of things.

It makes sense that it's on the other hand if you're an ad supported business, like agents aren't good for you because they're taking your content and then people are consuming it through their AI system and you're not getting eyeballs.
So you're the ad systems that work so we said like let's give everyone the tools to be able to stop it.
Let's create some scarcity in the supply that's out there.

And what we've seen is if you go across the biggest publishers in the world Conde Nast, Dotdash Meredith, you know they are they because we've now given them these tools they're actually signing much better deals with the AI companies and so that's I think that's sort of the first step but we've got to go beyond that because if you're a small blog or a you know influencer or a content creator like you don't have the ability to go strike the same deal that a Conde Nast does.

paragraphs.zh: 因此，我们提供工具，以尽可能低的成本、尽可能高的效率来获取信息，并把带着一堆冗余杂质的 HTML 转成 markdown（更精简的格式），这样一来，你就能在这些 AI 系统里塞进更多信息，而不会把它们的 context window（上下文窗口）撑爆，等等。这是说得通的。但另一方面，如果你是一个靠广告支持的业务，那 agent（智能代理）对你并不友好，因为它们会拿走你的内容，然后用户通过自己的 AI 系统来消费这些内容，你却得不到 eyeballs（用户注意力/曝光）。而广告系统靠的就是这个。所以我们说，要给所有人提供工具，让他们能够阻止这件事。让现有供给变得更稀缺一些。我们看到的是，如果你去看全球最大的那些出版商，Conde Nast、Dotdash Meredith，你会发现，因为我们现在给了他们这些工具，他们实际上正在和 AI 公司签下好得多的协议。所以我认为这算是第一步，但我们还得继续往前走，因为如果你是一个小 blog，或者一个 influencer、content creator，你并没有能力去谈到像 Conde Nast 那样的协议。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:18:20 - 1:19:01 There should be some basically a micro payment.
Every time we access the site and get information, we're happy to pay a fraction of a penny in order to do that.
That's harder than it sounds.
So we process in any given second about half a billion requests through our network.

We estimate that somewhere between 110% of those would be sort of monetizable through some micro payment transaction and so that means that you have to be able to support day one, call it 10,000,000 financial transactions per second with a clear ramp to be able to get to a 100,000,000 transactions per second.

paragraphs.zh: 原则上，应该有一种 micro payment（微支付）机制。每次我们访问一个网站并获取信息时，我们都愿意为此支付几分之一美分。只是这件事比听起来难得多。我们的网络在任意一秒大约会处理 5 亿次请求。我们估计，其中大约有 10% 会是那种可以通过某种 micro payment 交易来 monetizable（可货币化）的请求，这意味着你在第一天就必须能支持——姑且说——每秒 1000 万笔金融交易，而且还得有一条清晰的扩展路径，能够做到每秒 1 亿笔交易。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:19:01 - 1:19:43 Visa, which is the largest payments network in the world does fewer than a 100,000 transactions per second.
So we need to be, you know, two orders of magnitude bigger than Visa but with it much much smaller volumes.

And so we're working on that and we think that you know in partnership with with folks like Coinbase and Stripe, know, and and and protocols like x four zero two which is you know, it's interesting with the original Internet had something a response code for the 402.
We all know 404 which is not found and 500 which is an error.
Mean there are different things but 402 was payment required and we just never have really built anything around that.

paragraphs.zh: Visa 作为全球最大的支付网络，每秒处理的交易量也不到 10 万笔。所以我们需要做到比 Visa 大两个数量级，但对应的单笔金额却要小得多。我们正在推进这件事，而且我们认为，与 Coinbase、Stripe 这样的一些合作方，以及像 x four zero two 这样的 protocol（协议）合作，是可行的。这也很有意思：最初的 Internet 其实就有一个 402 的 response code（响应码）。我们都知道 404 是 not found，500 是 error，当然它们各有不同含义，但 402 代表的是 payment required，只是我们一直没有真正围绕它构建出什么东西。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:19:43 - 1:20:20 I think that's going to happen going forward.

And so, you know, Google really is responsible for having built the business model that's power the last twenty eight years of the Internet and I think we are trying to figure out, you know, what is that business model that's going to power the next twenty eight years of the internet but something has to pay content creators because they deserve to eat.
Something has to pay for the infrastructure that all of these bots are taxing effectively and I think we're working a lot around figuring out exactly what that looks like.

paragraphs.zh: 我觉得接下来这会发生。所以，Google 其实要为过去 28 年 Internet 商业模式的建立负责——正是它塑造了这个模式。而我认为，我们现在在试图弄清楚的是，什么样的商业模式会支撑未来 28 年的 Internet。但无论如何，必须有人为 content creator（内容创作者）付费，因为他们也要吃饭。也必须有人为这些 bots 实际上正在消耗、征税般压榨的基础设施买单。我认为我们正在做很多工作，想把这件事最终会是什么样子弄清楚。

sentences.text: Speaker 2 | 1:20:20 - 1:20:47 Great.
It's been an absolutely fascinating conversation.

The overarching thoughts as I was listening to you is, to which extent Cloudflare is becoming even more relevant today than it's been for the last seventeen years, which you built the network, and now the internet is changing and, you are absolutely at this sort of central point for it.
Yeah.
Is that how you how you guys think about it?

paragraphs.zh: 很好。这绝对是一场非常精彩的对话。我在听你说的时候，一个总体感受是：Cloudflare 在今天似乎比过去 17 年里的任何时候都更加重要了。你们先是建起了这个网络，而现在 Internet 正在发生变化，你们恰好处在这个变化的中心位置。对，这就是你们看待自己的方式吗？

sentences.text: Speaker 2 | 1:20:47 - 1:20:50 Like you're you're you have another seventeen years?

paragraphs.zh: 就像，你们还有下一个 17 年？

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:20:50 - 1:21:04 People are like, why, you know, why do you keep doing this?
There are days that aren't fun.
I mean laying off a bunch of people, I mean that wasn't fun.
And it's and you know I've made plenty of money.
So it's not about any of that.

paragraphs.zh: 人们会说，为什么，你知道，为什么你还要继续做这个？有些日子并不好过。我的意思是，裁掉一大批人，那肯定不好受。而且，你知道，我已经赚了很多钱了。所以这和那些都没关系。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:21:06 - 1:22:07 What it is about is is really our mission and we and we believe in that mission fundamentally and we we do things that that cost us money or or that certainly don't make us any money because we believe in helping build a better Internet.
And like I can't imagine anything that I could possibly be doing that is more rewarding than than figuring out how do we create some sort of sustainable business for the future.
I think there's a lot that's to be incredibly optimistic about.
The business model of the Internet for the last twenty eight years wasn't the most healthy.

Traffic has always been a terrible proxy for value and and and and so much of like the media landscape was how do you sort of define your narrow audience and then with a headline piss them off enough that they'll click on a link in order to you know drive eyeballs to your your site.

paragraphs.zh: 真正有关的是我们的使命，而且我们从根本上相信这个使命。我们会做一些让我们花钱的事，或者至少完全不赚钱的事，因为我们相信自己是在帮助建设一个更好的 Internet。而且我几乎想不到，还有什么事情能比去弄清楚：我们该如何为未来创造某种可持续的商业模式，更有成就感。我认为有很多事情是非常值得乐观的。过去 28 年 Internet 的商业模式并不是最健康的。traffic（流量）一直都是价值的一个糟糕代理指标，而媒体生态里有太多做法都是：你如何定义出一小群非常狭窄的受众，然后用一个标题把他们激怒到足以点开链接，从而把 eyeballs（用户注意力/曝光）导到你的网站上。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:22:08 - 1:22:46 And you know so much of this so much of what is dividing the world today is actually the underlying business model of the Internet which has been traffic based.
How do you drive as much much traffic as possible?
That's going away and I think that you know if we think about what can replace it going forward, it can be something which is much healthier and much much better.
I was I I flipped to Stockholm to to meet with Daniel Akk who who started Spotify and and because I mean like nobody has compensated creators at scale like Spotify.
It's just it's just it's remarkable.

paragraphs.zh: 你知道，如今世界上许多撕裂，实际上都源于互联网的底层商业模式——它一直是以 traffic（流量）为基础的。怎么尽可能地把更多更多的 traffic 拉进来？这种模式正在消退。我认为，如果我们去想未来有什么能取代它，那可以是某种更健康、也更好得多的东西。我之前飞去 Stockholm 见 Daniel Akk，他创办了 Spotify。因为说实话，没有谁像 Spotify 这样大规模地补偿 creators（创作者）。这真的非常了不起。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:22:46 - 1:23:31 When we can quibble about are the right people getting the money or not but but there's more money going into music creation today on on per capita, per GDP than any time in human history by far.

And and Spotify gets a lot of credit for that and and you forget that like twenty two years ago the music industry was dying, know, because on Napster and everything, everyone was just normalizing everyone stealing stealing content.
And then you know Steve Jobs steps on stage announces iTunes 99¢ a song.

That's not the business model that won but it at least planted a flag that said that there's there's something that's there and then I mean Spotify last year sent something like $12,000,000,000 out to music creators.
That's more than the that's more than the entire music industry's market cap was twenty two years ago, which is which is extraordinary.

paragraphs.zh: 我们当然可以争论，拿到钱的人是不是对的人，但无论如何，今天流向音乐创作的资金——按人均、按 GDP 比例来看——都远远超过人类历史上任何时候。而 Spotify 在这件事上功不可没。你会忘记，22 年前音乐产业其实快要死了，因为 Napster 之类的出现，让“偷内容”这件事被所有人渐渐视为正常。后来 Steve Jobs 上台，发布 iTunes，一首歌 99¢。这并不是最终胜出的商业模式，但它至少立起了一面旗帜，说明这里面是有路可走的。然后你看，Spotify 去年向音乐创作者分发了大约 $12,000,000,000。这比 22 年前整个音乐产业的 market cap（市值）还要高，真的非常惊人。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:23:31 - 1:24:08 And and so Daniel was telling the story.

He said one of the things that they that they do is if you search on Spotify for like Taylor Swift, Shake It Off, like they know they return results and they know that those results are what you were actually looking for.
On the other hand, you search for like I want a song to a disco beat about how much fun it is to dance with my cat.
They also return results but they know that that they probably don't have that and what they do is they actually publish those queries that are sort of bad results back to music creators.
And there are hundreds of music creators that are making a living off just doing this.

paragraphs.zh: Daniel 当时给我讲了一个故事。他说，他们做的一件事是：如果你在 Spotify 上搜索 Taylor Swift、Shake It Off 这样的内容，他们会返回结果，而且他们知道这些结果就是你真正想找的东西。另一方面，如果你搜索“我想要一首带 disco 节拍、讲和我的猫跳舞有多开心的歌”，他们也会返回结果，但他们知道自己大概率并没有这个东西。于是，他们会把这类结果不理想的查询重新发布给音乐创作者。现在已经有数百位音乐创作者，光靠做这件事就在谋生。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:24:08 - 1:24:37 One in particular is based in Denmark, Daniel shared is making something like €40,000,000 a year just writing songs for unfulfilled Spotify queries.
And I think there's something that's actually quite beautiful about that because if you search for like a something on Spotify, what you're searching for is how do I kind of bring an emotion out?
And and so going forward like we for the first time in human history have essentially a mathematical model of all of human knowledge.
That's what the LLMs are, right?
It's not perfect but it gives you that.

paragraphs.zh: Daniel 分享说，其中有一位在 Denmark，光是为 Spotify 上那些未被满足的搜索需求写歌，一年就能赚到大约 €40,000,000。我觉得这里面其实有一种相当美的东西。因为你在 Spotify 上搜索某样东西时，本质上是在寻找：我该怎样把某种情绪调动出来？而展望未来，我们在人类历史上第一次，基本拥有了一个关于全人类知识的 mathematical model（数学模型）。这就是 LLMs，对吧？它并不完美，但它确实给了你这样的东西。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:24:37 - 1:24:59 It also shows you where the gaps are, where there are the holes.
I picture like a giant block of Swiss cheese.
There's lots of cheese but there's a lot of holes in the cheese.
And when you talk to the AI companies, what they want is highly reputable sources that are filling in the holes in the cheese, are contributing net new knowledge.
They don't want yet another story about what happened at 1500 Pennsylvania Avenue, right?

paragraphs.zh: 它同时也会告诉你缺口在哪里、空白在哪里。我会把它想象成一大块 Swiss cheese。奶酪很多，但上面也有很多洞。你去和 AI 公司交流时，他们想要的是那些高度可信、能够把这些洞补上的 sources（来源），是那些能贡献 net new knowledge（全新净增知识）的内容。他们并不想再要一篇关于 1500 Pennsylvania Avenue 发生了什么的报道，对吧？

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:24:59 - 1:26:03 They want something where people are actually telling and creating new information that moves the world forward.

And I think that's what I want, I think that's what you want, I think that's what all of us are craving is how do we get back to sort of a media environment which isn't just trying to provoke outrage in order to drive traffic, but is actually trying to find the truth and fill that in.

And if we can create a system where the people who again are highly reputable and are creating sort of net new knowledge that those are the people that get rewarded, then I think that's incredibly incredibly beautiful.

What I keep pushing the AI labs to do is I'm like we should create the equivalent of I don't if it's the Academy Awards and Nobel Prize where we measure across a whole bunch of different segments who has contributed the most to human knowledge in the last year in you know I don't know cancer research and you know foreign policy news.

Mean you can create a bunch of these things and they can actually measure and say you know, this particular year the best podcast was you know the first Mark podcast that was that was out there or whatever it is.

paragraphs.zh: 他们想要的是那种人们真的在讲述、在创造新信息，并推动世界向前发展的东西。我觉得这正是我想要的，也是你想要的，也是我们所有人都在渴望的：我们怎样才能回到一种媒体环境，不是为了拉动 traffic 而刻意挑起愤怒，而是真正在努力寻找真相，并把那些空白填补起来。如果我们能建立一个系统，让那些再次强调一下——高度可信、并且在创造 net new knowledge 的人——得到回报，那我觉得这会是极其极其美好的事。我一直在推动 AI labs 去做的一件事是：我们应该建立某种相当于 Academy Awards 和 Nobel Prize 的东西——我不确定具体像哪一个——在很多不同领域里衡量，过去一年谁对人类知识贡献最大，比如我不知道，cancer research，以及 foreign policy news。你完全可以建立很多这样的评选机制，真的去衡量并说：比如某一年，最好的 podcast 是第一期 Mark podcast，或者不管具体是什么。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:26:03 - 1:26:27 And we should be rewarding and celebrating both both in terms of honoring these people but then also paying them for doing that.
And I think if we do that, you're going to get a lot more, you know, interesting information.
My wife and I bought the local newspaper in our in our hometown Park City, Utah.
I think we will make more this year off AI licensing deals than we do off display ads.
Why?

paragraphs.zh: 而且我们应该去奖励、去赞颂这些人——不仅仅是给予荣誉，也要真的为他们的工作付费。我觉得如果我们这样做，你就会得到多得多的有趣信息。我和我妻子买下了我们家乡 Utah 州 Park City 的本地报纸。我认为我们今年从 AI licensing deals（AI 授权交易）中赚到的钱，会比 display ads（展示广告）还多。为什么？

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:26:27 - 1:27:28 Because if you're planning a vacation to go skiing in Park City, Utah, you want to know what's the best hotel to stay at, what's the best restaurant, who's going to be performing, know, what are the snow conditions like and and the only place that has that is the local newspaper.

And so while the last twenty eight years of the internet kind of killed local quirky unique interesting information, what I'm hopeful is that as we figure out what the business model is for the next twenty eight years it actually brings a lot of those things back and and it actually gets back to let's talk about facts, let's talk about reality and okay I like the New York Times, be amazing if they were covering like in the Marriott Marquis hotel in in Times Square.
Room 1313 is better than Room 1314 or if you know if you're if you want to sleep in late room you know 1314 faces north and so it's less likely to get sun in the morning.
Like those are the sorts of here's the best bodega in New York.
Very interesting quirky local stories.

paragraphs.zh: 因为如果你正计划去 Utah 州 Park City 滑雪度假，你会想知道：住哪家 hotel 最好、哪家 restaurant 最好、谁会来演出、雪况怎么样，而唯一真正掌握这些信息的地方，就是本地报纸。所以，尽管过去 28 年的互联网某种程度上扼杀了本地的、古怪的、独特的、有趣的信息，我所希望的是，当我们弄清楚未来 28 年的商业模式时，它反而会把很多这样的东西带回来，带我们回到“来谈事实吧，来谈现实吧”的状态。比如说，我喜欢 the New York Times，但如果他们能去报道 Times Square 的 Marriott Marquis hotel 里，1313 房间比 1314 房间更好，或者如果你想睡到晚一点，1314 朝北，早上被太阳晒到的概率更低——那会非常棒。又比如“这是 New York 最好的 bodega”。就是这种非常有意思、很有地方特色、很古怪的本地故事。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:27:28 - 1:27:40 I think that that is both a media future and an internet future that we all crave and want and I think it's one that we actually have an opportunity to build because we get to reinvent what the business model is going be.

paragraphs.zh: 我认为，那既是我们都渴望、都想要的一种媒体未来，也是一种互联网未来；而且我认为，这也是我们确实有机会去构建的未来，因为我们可以重新定义未来的商业模式会是什么样。

sentences.text: Speaker 2 | 1:27:40 - 1:27:45 Fascinating.
Well Matthew, thank you so much.
This was an absolutely wonderful conversation.
Really appreciate it.

paragraphs.zh: 很精彩。那么 Matthew，真的非常感谢你。这绝对是一场非常棒的对话。非常感谢。

sentences.text: Speaker 1 | 1:27:45 - 1:27:46 Thank you for having me.

paragraphs.zh: 感谢邀请我来。

sentences.text: Speaker 2 | 1:27:48 - 1:28:07 Hi it's Matt Turk again, thanks for listening to this episode of the MAD Podcast.

If you enjoyed it, we'd be very grateful if you would consider subscribing if you haven't already or leaving a positive review or comment on whichever platform you're watching this or listening to this episode from.
This really helps us build a podcast and get great guests.
Thanks and see you on the next episode.

paragraphs.zh: 大家好，我是 Matt Turk，再次感谢你收听这一期 MAD Podcast。如果你喜欢这一期内容，而你还没有订阅的话，我们将非常感激你考虑订阅，或者在你观看或收听本期节目的平台上留下积极的评价或评论。这对我们打造这个 podcast 并邀请到优秀嘉宾真的很有帮助。谢谢，我们下期再见。
